main_sections.title: Magnifying Glass
main_sections.poem_text:
With this round glass
I can make Magic talk—
A myriad shells show
In a scrap of chalk;
Of but an inch of moss
A forest—flowers and trees;
A drop of water
Like hive of bees.

I lie in wait and watch
How the deft spider jets
The woven web-silk
From his spinnerets;
The tigerish claws he has!
And oh! the silly flies
The stumble into his net—
With all those eyes!

Not even the tiniest thing
But this my glass
Will make more marvellous
And itself surpass.

Yes, and with lenses like it,
Eyeing the moon,
'Twould seem you'd walk there
In an afternoon!

Walter de la Mare
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Magnifying Glass by Walter de la Mare celebrates the transformative power of close observation through a simple lens. The speaker delights in how the magnifying glass turns the ordinary into the extraordinary: a scrap of chalk reveals a myriad of shells, an inch of moss becomes a forest, a drop of water teems like a hive of bees, and a spider's web becomes an intricate, almost predatory marvel complete with tigerish claws and multifaceted eyes. The tone is one of wonder and playful curiosity, with exclamations and vivid imagery sustaining a child-like sense of amazement throughout. The poem employs simile ('Like hive of bees'), metaphor ('Magic talk'), alliteration ('woven web-silk'), and personification to animate the world seen through the glass. The final stanza extends the metaphor from tiny objects to the celestial — lenses like the magnifying glass could bring the moon within walking distance — suggesting that scientific tools infinitely expand human perception and possibility.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Work in pairs. Answer the questions that follow the pictures given below. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: What is common in all these pictures?
Select the pictures you are familiar with.
Why do you think the people in the pictures need to use a magnifying glass?
What other instruments magnify objects?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: If you have a magnifying glass, what would you like to use it for and why? Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.
Complete the summary of the poem given below with suitable words from the poem.

question_bank.stem: The poem describes the magic of seeing the world through a 1. __________. It reveals how tiny things like 2. __________ and 3. __________ can appear vast and complex. Even a 4. __________ of water can seem like a hive of 5. __________. The poet is impressed at how the spider spins its 6. __________ from its 7. __________. The poem ends with the idea that, through lenses, the 8. __________ could seem within reach.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct answer from the brackets.

question_bank.stem: The main idea of the poem is the transformative power of __________ (wonders in nature/close observation) through the magnifying glass.
The tone of the poem is __________. (wonder and curiosity/peaceful and emotional)
The poem has __________ stanzas with __________ lines in each stanza. Hence, it is a quatrain. (four; six/six; four)
The rhyme scheme of the poem is __________. (ABCD; ABCB)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Pick examples from the poem for the following poetic devices.

question_bank.stem: Pick an example of Simile from the poem.
Pick an example of Alliteration from the poem.
Pick an example of Metaphor from the poem.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The poem is rich in visual imagery, painting vivid pictures of small, everyday things magnified into something grand.

question_bank.stem: In the line, 'A myriad shells show in a scrap of chalk', the magnifying glass reveals ______________________.
In the line, 'A forest—flowers and trees' the poet uses the imagery of nature to emphasise ______________________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the following sentences with a reason.

question_bank.stem: The poet uses exclamation marks in lines, 'The tigerish claws he has!', 'With all those eyes!' and 'In an afternoon!' because it ______________________.
In the phrase, 'Magic talk' the poet uses personification to describe the magnifying glass because it ______________________.

Each stanza follows a repetitive pattern of introducing a small or ordinary object and then describing the extraordinary details revealed through magnification. This is because it supports the theme of __________ as the speaker reveals ____________.
In the final stanza the poet shifts from small, everyday objects being magnified to a celestial body like moon because __________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: With this round glass / I can make Magic talk— / A myriad shells show / In a scrap of chalk; / Of but an inch of moss / A forest—flowers and trees;

question_bank.stem: Identify whether the following statement is true or false. The poet uses his magical powers to make the round glass powerful.
Select the line from the extract that expresses the presence of intricate patterns in ordinary objects.
What does the comparison of 'an inch of moss' to a 'forest' suggest about the speaker's view of the world through the magnifying glass?

How does the poet feel about the ability of the magnifying glass to reveal hidden wonders?
A. Satisfied  B. Fascinated  C. Grateful  D. Determined

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What is the significance of the spider in the poem?
How might the speaker's view of the natural world change if there was no use of a magnifying glass?
Why does the poem end with the idea of the moon being within reach?
What is the speaker's attitude towards nature and the act of observation?
Which is your favourite part of the poem? Why?

listening.transcript_text:
Daughter: Papa, everyone talks about how yoga and the concept of zero came from India, but I recently found out there are so many more inventions that people don't really know about!
Father: Oh really? Like what?
Daughter: Well, did you know that radio broadcasting has roots in India? We usually credit Marconi, but Jagadish Chandra Bose, a Bengali scientist, actually conducted an experiment using microwaves before Marconi.
Father: Wow, I didn't know that! That's incredible. I can recollect something about fibre optics.
Daughter: Yes! You're absolutely right Papa. Can you imagine life without fibre optics? No fast internet, no clear communication… And guess what? It was pioneered by Narinder Kapany, an Indian physicist from Punjab. He's called the 'father of fibre optics.'
Father: That's something I use every day, but I just had an idea that the origins were in India. What about fun things? Any of those that you've found out about?
Daughter: Yes indeed! You'll love this one—Snakes and Ladders! It was actually invented as a game to teach children values, with ladders representing virtues and snakes representing evil. It wasn't just a board game like we think today. It had a spiritual meaning in ancient times.
Father: So even that simple game had such deep roots. Let me tell you about one that I just remembered.
Daughter: Of course! I'd love to know.
Father: Well, the USB port, something we rely on all the time to connect devices, was invented by Ajay Bhatt. He's an Indian-born engineer who helped revolutionise the way we use technology.
Daughter: That's amazing. USB is such a basic part of life now. It's hard to imagine a time without it.
Father: That's incredible. And all this from India. I definitely learned a lot from this conversation.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between a father and daughter about lesser-known Indian inventions. As you listen, mark the four true statements from (1)–(6) given below.

question_bank.stem: The father is surprised by the fact that India was involved in the invention of radio broadcasting and fibre optics.
The daughter appears indifferent when learning the origins of the USB port.
The father shows little interest in the information about the origin of snakes and ladders.
The father was simply confirming all the facts shared by the daughter.
The daughter expresses pride and admiration for India's role in global inventions.
The daughter is excited as she shares new discoveries about India's contributions with her father.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
When pronouncing /v/ and /f/, the lower lip lightly touches the edge of the upper teeth. Note the difference between /v/ and /f/. Read these words aloud with the help of your teacher.
beginning sound /f/: forest, flowers, from
middle sound /f/: deft, afternoon
end sound /f/: of, itself
beginning sound /v/: vase, valour, victory
middle sound /v/: woven, even
end sound /v/: hive, marvellous
If you could travel to space, which planet would you like to visit? Why? What preparations would you like to make? Speak about any five things that you would like to take along.
If you could invent something, what would you like to invent and how? Give reasons for the choice of your invention. Speak about your wonder invention.

In a conversation, we communicate through dialogues. Deepa and Asma, members of the Science Club have a conversation to take a decision about making a model for an upcoming Science Fair. Create this conversation between Deepa and Asma.
Points to remember: mention where, when, and with whom the conversation is taking place; identify word choice and tone—formal or informal; include key information, points of agreement and disagreement; use words in brackets to express emotions or actions; use filler words like Uff, Er…, Ugh etc.
You may begin: Deepa: Hi Asma! Our Science teacher mentioned that you have a wonderful idea for the Science Fair project. Asma: (hesitatingly) Yes, I do. But I'm not sure if it is good enough. Deepa: (encouragingly) Don't worry…

Fill in the blanks in the sentences with the words given in the box below: woven, myriad, deft, stumble, surpass, marvellous.
With his 1. ____________ description, the author has written the story beautifully 2. ____________ with fascinating details. When you read the story, you will surely enjoy the 3. ____________ storytelling technique that attracts all kinds of readers. What makes the story interesting is the superb way of describing the 4. ____________ emotions and feelings of the characters. The reader will 5. ____________ upon the unexpected twists and turns that 6. ____________ all our understanding and make us wonder at the narrative power of the author.

question_bank.stem: Fill: With his 1. ____________ description, the author has written the story beautifully 2. ____________ with fascinating details. When you read the story, you will surely enjoy the 3. ____________ storytelling technique that attracts all kinds of readers. What makes the story interesting is the superb way of describing the 4. ____________ emotions and feelings of the characters. The reader will 5. ____________ upon the unexpected twists and turns that 6. ____________ all our understanding.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
The collective noun 'a hive of bees' is used in the poem. Match the phrases in Column 1 with suitable words in Column 2 to make collective nouns.
1. a swarm of — (iv) locusts
2. a constellation of — (vi) stars
3. a grove of — (v) trees
4. a troupe of — (iii) dancers
5. a battalion of — (ii) soldiers
6. a fleet of — (i) ships

question_bank.stem: Match collective noun phrases to the correct words: a swarm of ___, a constellation of ___, a grove of ___, a troupe of ___, a battalion of ___, a fleet of ___. (options: ships, soldiers, dancers, locusts, trees, stars)

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
The poet uses the expression 'eyeing the moon' in the poem. Match the idiomatic expressions with 'eye' given in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. You may refer to a dictionary.
1. apple of one's eye — (v) a person who is very precious or important
2. in the blink of an eye — (iii) something that happens very quickly
3. keep an eye on something or somebody — (i) watch something or someone closely
4. turn a blind eye — (ii) act as if you do not see or notice
5. see eye to eye — (vi) agree with each other
6. bird's-eye view — (iv) an overall look at something

question_bank.stem: Match 'eye' idioms to their meanings: apple of one's eye, in the blink of an eye, keep an eye on, turn a blind eye, see eye to eye, bird's-eye view.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The poet uses the adjective form 'tigerish' in the poem by adding the suffix -'ish' to the noun 'tiger'. Choose the nouns to which you can add the suffix -'ish' to make adjectives: girl, glass, book, silk, boy, water, moon, scrap.

question_bank.stem: Choose the nouns from the list that can take the suffix '-ish' to make adjectives: girl, glass, book, silk, boy, water, moon, scrap.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Children with low vision may benefit from different types of visual aids, such as magnifying spectacles, stand magnifiers, hand-held magnifiers, and telescopes. Read about the different types and their uses.

Did you know that telescopes also use lenses? Read about: (1) the largest telescope in India in Nainital, Uttarakhand (ARIES, 2016); (2) the Indian Astronomical Observatory (IAO) in Hanle, Ladakh at 4,500m elevation; (3) the James Webb Space Telescope (NASA, Dec 2021, 1.5 million km from Earth); (4) Mauna Kea Observatory in Hawaii. Find out more about them from the internet and discuss with your science teacher and classmates.

Read about ancient Indian scholars: (1) Āryabhaṭa — Āryabhaṭiyam, heliocentric model, lunar and solar eclipses; (2) Varāmihira — Pañchasiddhāntika, sixth century CE; (3) Bhāskarachārya II — Siddhāntaśiromaṇi and Karaṇakutūhala, tenth century CE.

Read about Jantar Mantar in New Delhi — an astronomical observatory built in 1724 by Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh II, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Five observatories were built in Ujjain, Mathura, Varanasi, Jaipur, and New Delhi.
